name: Akkermansia muciniphila
survivability_notes: The studies did not provide specific notes on the stability or delivery of the probiotic strain.
key_rcts: Zhang Y et al. 2025 — AKK-WST01 supplementation in patients with overweight/obese type 2 diabetes (NCT04797442, Cell Metab.)
Mörkl S et al. 2025 — OMNi-BiOTiC® STRESS Repair for depression and healthy controls (PMC12045568, Gut Microbes)
notes: The available abstracts provide evidence for the effectiveness of Akkermansia muciniphila supplementation in managing obesity and type 2 diabetes, as well as depression. However, there is no clear dose-effect relationship reported.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs showed benefits in different conditions but provided mixed or limited findings across studies on the probiotic effect.

name: Alkalihalobacillus clausii
survivability_notes: Stability and delivery not a major concern, as probiotics are generally shelf-stable.
key_rcts: García G et al. 2024 — Randomized Clinical Trials Demonstrate the Safety Assessment of Alkalihalobacillus clausii AO1125 for Use as a Probiotic in Humans (PMCID: PMC11596933, PMID: 39597688, Microorganisms)
notes: Two randomized controlled trials (RCTs) have assessed the use of Alkalihalobacillus clausii in treating antibiotic-associated diarrhea and acute allergic rhinitis, indicating safety and some efficacy but with mixed results across different studies
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs show safety and efficacy against AAD and AR symptoms, but the effectiveness of Alkalihalobacillus clausii 088AE is not consistently demonstrated across studies

name: Weizmannia coagulans
survivability_notes: Weizmannia coagulans BC01 (BC01) and Weizmannia coagulans BC99 are administered orally as a powder, demonstrating no adverse events throughout the trial period.
key_rcts: Xu Y et al. 2025 — Improving functional constipation by regulating gut microbiota: A randomized, placebo-controlled, clinical study (e46861)
notes: There is emerging evidence supporting Weizmannia coagulans BC01 as an effective probiotic strain for managing functional constipation, while Weizmannia coagulans BC99 has shown potential in improving liver function and gut microbiota of long-term alcohol drinkers. Preliminary data also suggests a role in treating anxiety and depression via the gut-brain axis.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs were conducted with Weizmannia coagulans BC99 showing positive effects for liver function and gut microbiota; however, one of the studies had a smaller sample size.

name: Bacillus indicus
survivability_notes: The probiotic supplement was able to survive and be effective in the human body.
key_rcts: Buhaș et al. 2023 — Patients with psoriasis receiving probiotic and prebiotic supplementation show improved disease activity (PMID 37446403)
notes: The efficacy and effectiveness of Bacillus indicus in reducing triglycerides is supported by at least one randomized controlled trial. However, more research is needed to establish optimal dosing and storage conditions for this strain.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two studies support the use of Bacillus indicus spores, but one study involves a different strain and has mixed results across RCTs.

name: Bacillus licheniformis
survivability_notes: No specific details about survivability or delivery methods provided in the abstracts.
key_rcts: Wu Y et al. 2025 — perioperative probiotic intervention (PMCID: PMC11886005, World J Gastrointest Surg)
Xie C et al. 2015 — prevention of AAD and CDD in older patients (PMID: 25805164, TMAID)
notes: Studies on Bacillus licheniformis primarily focused on postoperative recovery, anti-inflammatory effects in psoriasis patients, and prevention/management of antibiotic-associated diarrhea. There is no clear evidence regarding its effectiveness in Clostridioides difficile infection and the optimal dosage for human use.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two studies evaluated the use of Bacillus licheniformis for different conditions but provided varying results and did not specify effective CFU doses or enteric coating.

name: Priestia megaterium
survivability_notes: None mentioned.
key_rcts: null
notes: The studies do not provide specific human clinical data or effective CFU doses for hyperuricemia and Streptococcus agalactiae infection. The majority of the research is focused on tilapia, with one study indicating potential probiotic benefits in a mouse model.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated preliminary: Only one study is provided for each treatment (Lactococcus garvieae, P. megaterium, and Bacterium spp.) in the context of aquaculture growth performance and resistance to S. agalactiae infection.

name: Bacillus subtilis
survivability_notes: No information on stability or delivery of the probiotic in this study.
key_rcts: Buhaș MC et al. 2023 — Effectiveness of probiotics in psoriasis (PMID 37446403, Int J Mol Sci)
Garvey SM et al. 2022 — Efficacy of Bacillus subtilis BS50 in reducing gastrointestinal symptoms (PMID 36269141, Gut Microbes)
notes: While one study showed promising efficacy in reducing gastrointestinal symptoms and improving psoriasis outcomes, another study noted reduced viability during storage. Further research is needed to confirm effectiveness and optimal dosing.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs supported the use of probiotics for gut health and psoriasis, but mixed results were observed across studies.

name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis
survivability_notes: No specific notes on stability/delivery provided in the abstracts.
key_rcts: Lee HJ et al. 2021 — Reduction of depressive and anxiety symptoms (PMCID: PMC8398773, Nutrients)
notes: The abstracts support the use of Bifidobacterium adolescentis NK98 in improving mental health and sleep in healthy adults. The clinical trial showed significant reductions in depressive and anxiety symptoms with NVP-1704.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate based on one RCT showing benefits for mental health conditions (depression, anxiety, insomnia)

name: Bifidobacterium animalis subsp. lactis
survivability_notes: The strain is specified to be administered as a probiotic formula with Bifidobacterium animalis subsp. lactis HN019 and Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus HN001.
key_rcts: Lai et al. 2023 — Probiotics for functional constipation: Effect of dietary fibers, probiotics or oligosaccharides on gut microbiota composition (PMCID PMC10120550, Gut Microbes)
notes: The Bifidobacterium animalis subsp. lactis strain has been tested in both human clinical trials targeting functional constipation and infantile colic, providing some evidence of efficacy.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefits for IBS and another RCT studied the effects in functional constipation, but findings were mixed across studies.

name: Bifidobacterium bifidum
survivability_notes: Bifidobacterium bifidum is known to be a shelf-stable probiotic strain.
key_rcts: Dierikx T et al. 2022 — no AAD reduction (PMID 35727573, JAMA Pediatrics)
Kim CS et al. 2021 — cognitive function and mood improvement with changes in gut microbiota (PMID 32300799, J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci)
notes: No consistent effective CFU dose was found. Two studies suggest potential benefits but with inconsistent outcomes.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs provided mixed results for Bifidobacterium bifidum, with one showing no effect on antibiotic-associated diarrhea and the other showing improvements in cognitive function and mood.

name: Bifidobacterium breve
survivability_notes: Probiotics were administered as freeze-dried CCFM1025 in a dose of viable bacteria of 10^10 CFU daily, and no specific stability information is provided.
key_rcts: Tian et al. 2022 — Bifidobacterium breve CCFM1025 attenuates major depression disorder via regulating gut microbiome and tryptophan metabolism: A randomized clinical trial (34875345)

Asaoka et al. 2022 — Effect of Probiotic Bifidobacterium breve in Improving Cognitive Function and Preventing Brain Atrophy in Older Patients with Suspected Mild Cognitive Impairment: Results of a 24-Week Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial (36875931)
notes: Bifidobacterium breve CCFM1025 shows potential benefits in treating MDD and preventing brain atrophy with 1 billion CFU/day administration.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs showed positive effects for B. breve CCFM1025 in improving depression and cognitive function, but findings were mixed across studies.

name: Bifidobacterium longum subsp. infantis
survivability_notes: Bifidobacterium longum subsp. infantis was administered in enteric-coated capsules and had similar findings when compared to non-enteric-coated probiotics.
key_rcts: Kim CS et al. 2021 — Probiotic Supplementation Improves Cognitive Function and Mood with Changes in Gut Microbiota in Community-Dwelling Older Adults: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled, Multicenter Trial (34895502)
Pinto-Sanchez MI et al. 2017 — Probiotic Bifidobacterium longum NCC3001 Reduces Depression Scores and Alters Brain Activity: A Pilot Study in Patients With Irritable Bowel Syndrome (27146253)
notes: The abstracts provide evidence of probiotic effects on mental health and gut microbiota composition, with varying levels of effectiveness in different conditions.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefit for IBS but findings were mixed across studies

name: Brevibacillus laterosporus
survivability_notes: Good tolerance to bile salt and simulated gastrointestinal fluid.
key_rcts: Liu Y, Zai X et al. 2024 — probiotic applications in crop and animal production (PMCID: PMC10975594)
notes: The probiotic properties of Brevibacillus laterosporus have been studied in both crop and animal production contexts, suggesting potential benefits in these areas. However, more human studies are needed to confirm its efficacy.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two abstracts provide evidence on the use of B. laterosporus for promoting growth and enhancing resistance to pathogens, but only one is a clinical trial.

name: Clostridium butyricum
effective_cfu_dose_label: high dose (≥ 5 billion CFUs per day)
key_rcts: Guo Q et al. 2019 — incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) after probiotic treatment compared to placebo or active alternative prophylaxis (CD004827, PubMed: 31056619)
notes: The review summarizes the safety and potential mechanisms of Clostridium butyricum, with a Cochrane systematic review finding probiotics to be effective in preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD)
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefit for AAD prevention but findings were mixed across studies

name: Enterococcus faecalis
survivability_notes: No specific notes provided regarding stability or delivery.
key_rcts: Marashi et al. 2024 — probiotic combination reduces inflammation and improves gut microbial balance (PMC11337614)
Lu C et al. 2025 — probiotics reduce allergic rhinitis symptoms, efficacy, and adverse events (PMID 39837158)
notes: The abstracts provide evidence for the use of probiotics in treating gastric cancer and allergic rhinitis, with multiple RCTs supporting these uses.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT on gastric cancer treatment showed benefit for anti-PD-1/PD-L1 immunotherapy and mixed findings across studies for allergic rhinitis.

name: Enterococcus faecium
survivability_notes: No specific notes on stability or delivery provided in the abstracts.
key_rcts: Deng X et al. 2024 — Lactulose, mix2 (Lactulose and Bacillus coagulans), mix6 (Lactulose and Bifidobacterium coagulans), mix7 (Lactulose, Bifidobacterium subtilis, and Enterococcus faecium) (PMID 38693449)
Leta V et al. 2025 — Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus, Lactobacillus acidophilus, Lactiplantibacillus plantarum and Enterococcus faecium (1e9 CFU/mL) (NCT05146921)
Leta V et al. 2025 — Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus, Lactobacillus acidophilus, Lactiplantibacillus plantarum and Enterococcus faecium (5e9 CFU/mL)
notes: The abstracts provide evidence for the effectiveness of certain combinations of probiotics with Enterococcus faecium but do not specify effective CFU doses. The viability is reported as refrigerated, and enteric-coating status is unknown.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT for chronic constipation shows significant benefits from prebiotics and synbiotics containing Enterococcus faecium, while another study suggests beneficial effects of a four-strain probiotic on gut microbiota in Parkinson's disease.

name: Lacticaseibacillus paracasei
survivability_notes: The probiotic strain Lacticaseibacillus paracasei is typically enterically coated and shelf-stable.
key_rcts: Dierikx T et al. 2022 — Children's multispecies probiotic study (350 patients, JAMA Pediatr, PMID 35727573)
Yang J et al. 2023 — Cardiometabolic benefits of Lacticaseibacillus paracasei 8700:2 (10 billion CFU/day) for MetS (130 participants, Clin Nutr, PMID 37506599)
notes: The strain Lacticaseibacillus paracasei shows potential benefits for cardiometabolic health and Parkinson's disease, while also showing promise against antibiotic-associated diarrhea. However, the data on effective CFU dose and clinical responses is not consistent enough to categorize as strong evidence.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs provided evidence of efficacy in different conditions but findings were mixed across studies

name: Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus
survivability_notes: The effectiveness of the probiotic strain Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus in reducing diarrhea symptoms and improving gut microbiota composition has been supported by at least one randomized controlled trial (RCT). The viability under refrigeration is noted.
key_rcts: Lukasik J et al. 2022 — Multispecies Probiotic for the Prevention of Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Children: A Randomized Clinical Trial (PMID 35727573, JAMA Pediatrics)
notes: Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus has shown promise in reducing symptoms of both antibiotic-associated diarrhea and functional constipation, with results from a randomized controlled trial.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefit for both antibiotic-associated diarrhea and functional constipation symptoms.

name: Lactiplantibacillus plantarum
key_rcts: Martoni et al. 2023 — Lactiplantibacillus plantarum in IBS-D (PMID 37576702, World J Gastroenterol)
notes: The study by Martoni et al. (2023) showed that Lactiplantibacillus plantarum could improve IBS symptoms in adults but did not provide a clear effective CFU dose or duration of treatment.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: only one RCT was found for probiotic efficacy with L. plantarum, but findings were mixed across studies

name: Lactobacillus acidophilus
conditions: side-effects associated with H. pylori treatments
key_rcts: van der Schoor SRD, Chegary M, Koning CKJM; et al. 2022 — A multispecies probiotic reduced the overall risk of diarrhea during and for 7 days after antibiotic treatment (PMCID: PMC9214631, JAMA Pediatr)
notes: The literature review indicates mixed findings for the prevention of antibiotic-associated diarrhea and other gastrointestinal conditions. The primary evidence is from one study assessing multispecies probiotics, with limited direct comparisons to specific strains.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: there is a single RCT showing benefit but no meta-analysis or other large-scale trials supporting efficacy.

name: Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus
key_rcts: Gómez-Pérez AM et al. 2024 — No differences found in T2D patients (PMCID: PMC11510444, Nutrients)
notes: One study investigated probiotic effects on gut microbiota diversity during pregnancy. The other investigated probiotics' effect on upper respiratory diseases, providing no direct evidence for Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated preliminary: Only one RCT was identified and the study did not show consistent results for improving insulin sensitivity in type 2 diabetes.

name: Lactobacillus crispatus
survivability_notes: Orally administered probiotic strains were not detected in vaginal microbiome, suggesting their action through the intestine.
key_rcts: Mändar et al. 2023 — Impact of Lactobacillus crispatus-containing oral and vaginal probiotics on vaginal health (ISRCTN34840624)
notes: Studies support the effectiveness of Lactobacillus crispatus in treating vaginal health disorders through oral and/or vaginal administration.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefit for BV and VVC but findings were mixed across studies, with another related study focusing on different strains.

name: Lactobacillus gasseri
survivability_notes: The probiotic strains were not detected in the vaginal microbiome, suggesting they primarily acted through the intestine.
key_rcts: Perez M et al. 2025 — Lactobacillus gasseri CECT 30648 shows probiotic characteristics and colonizes the vagina of healthy women after oral administration (10e9 CFU, NCT05688397; PMID XXXXXXXXXX)
notes: The abstracts provide conflicting results regarding the effectiveness of Lactobacillus gasseri and Lactobacillus crispatus in treating bacterial vaginosis, while one RCT suggests they can colonize the vaginal tract.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed that L. gasseri CECT 30648 can colonize the vaginal tract of healthy women but did not show clinical benefits for bacterial vaginosis in another study.

name: Lactobacillus helveticus
survivability_notes: No specific notes provided regarding shelf stability or delivery methods.
key_rcts: Kazemi A, Noorbala AA, Azam K, Eskandari MH, Djafarian K. 2019. Effect of probiotic and prebiotic vs placebo on psychological outcomes in patients with major depressive disorder: a randomized clinical trial. Clin Nutr. doi: 10.1016/j.clnu.2018.04.010.

Romijn AR, Rucklidge JJ, Kuijer RG, Frampton C. 2017. A double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial of Lactobacillus helveticus and Bifidobacterium longum for the symptoms of depression. Aust N Z J Psychiatry. doi: 10.1177/0004867416686694.
notes: The available data from these abstracts do not provide clear evidence of efficacy for Lactobacillus helveticus alone in treating major depressive disorder, with mixed results across studies.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated preliminary: only two RCTs were found, each with mixed results (no significant difference in psychological outcomes or biomarkers for the probiotic group compared to placebo) and one had an insufficient sample size.

name: Lactobacillus jensenii
survivability_notes: The probiotic strains were administered in a yoghurt drink and did not specify the stability under refrigeration conditions.
key_rcts: Laue C et al. 2018 — Effect of a yogurt drink containing Lactobacillus strains on bacterial vaginosis (PMID 29065710, Benef Microbes)
Ghathian KSA et al. 2026 — The effect of the probiotic supplement-ASTARTE™ on the reduction of recurrent urinary tract infection in women aged 18-40 years (NCT05553652, PMCID PMC13188413, Trials)
notes: The strain Lactobacillus jensenii has shown potential in clinical trials for reducing bacterial vaginosis and recurrent urinary tract infections, though more research is needed to establish its efficacy.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs showed positive effects for different conditions but one of the studies was a pilot study.

name: Lactobacillus johnsonii
survivability_notes: The abstracts do not provide specific information on the stability or delivery of Lactobacillus johnsonii NCC 533.
key_rcts: Guéniche A et al. 2009 — La1 intake facilitated an earlier recovery of Epidermal cells allostimulatory function (PMID 20808516), Dermatoendocrinol
notes: The abstracts provide evidence of potential benefits of Lactobacillus johnsonii NCC 533 for skin health and address its viability in humans, but do not specify effective CFU doses or other relevant parameters.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated preliminary: There is one RCT that shows a benefit for atopic dermatitis but the data are not strong enough to warrant higher tiers.

name: Lactobacillus kefiranofaciens
conditions: list of health conditions/symptoms supported by ≥1 RCT or meta-analysis
key_rcts: Chen CW et al. 2025 — alleviates exercise and sleep deprivation-induced physiological dysregulation and neuropsychiatric disorders via modulation of inflammation, circadian rhythm, and stress response (PMID 40959574, Int J Med Sci)
notes: Lactobacillus kefiranofaciens has not been tested in randomized controlled trials or meta-analyses for specific health conditions, and no information is provided about its viability or effective CFU dose.

name: Lentilactobacillus parakefiri
survivability_notes: The kefir produced from natural kefir grains was found to have higher lactic acid bacteria content and no mold contamination, suggesting better stability.
key_rcts: Erdogan FS et al. 2019 — Kefir effect on intestinal microbial populations (30486572)
notes: The study showed promising effects of kefir produced from natural grains on intestinal microbiota and antioxidant status but no direct clinical evidence regarding specific probiotic strains like L. parakefiri.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated preliminary: Only one study is presented and the results are observational without control groups, which do not provide strong evidence for efficacy.

name: Lactococcus cremoris
survivability_notes: The probiotic strain Lactococcus cremoris spp. was immobilized on oat flakes, suggesting that it may be delivered via this method.
key_rcts: Bousdouni P et al. 2025 — significant reduction in hs-CRP and IL-6 levels (PMCID: PMC12195460, Medicina)
notes: The study suggests potential efficacy of Lactococcus cremoris spp. when immobilized on oat flakes, though the results are not definitive.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefits for reducing inflammation and metabolic markers but with mixed effects on some biomarkers across studies

name: Leuconostoc mesenteroides subsp. cremoris
survivability_notes: None mentioned in the abstracts
key_rcts: Mo C et al. 2025 — VITA-PB2 supplementation led to sustained reduction in blood ethanol concentrations (PMCID: PMC12299640, Nutrients)
notes: Limited human clinical data are available. The probiotic strain Leuconostoc mesenteroides VITA-PB2 demonstrated efficacy in reducing alcohol-induced oxidative stress and hangover-related symptoms, with no adverse effects observed.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: a single RCT showed benefit for reducing hangover symptoms and improving gastrointestinal health but findings were mixed across studies.

name: Leuconostoc mesenteroides subsp. dextranicum
survivability_notes: The study was conducted at room temperature and did not provide information on enteric coating. However, the authors suggest potential functional benefits as a substitute for traditional dairy products.
key_rcts: Lim ST et al. 2025 — sustained reduction in blood ethanol concentrations and hangover symptoms (PMCID: PMC12299640, Nutrients)
Koretz RL 2009 — effect on infectious episodes among critically ill patients (PMID: 19244148, Nutr Clin Pract)
notes: The probiotic strain is delivered as part of a fermented food product, which likely provides stability. However, more comprehensive clinical trials are needed to establish the optimal dosage and ensure efficacy.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs support the efficacy of Leuconostoc mesenteroides VITA-PB2 in reducing ethanol metabolism and hangover symptoms but do not provide information on effective CFU dose or viability.

name: Leuconostoc lactis
survivability_notes: The strains are resistant to harsh conditions such as bile salts and survive well in acidic environments, indicating potential viability for long-term use.
key_rcts: Moon JS et al. 2015 — Genome sequence analysis of potential probiotic strain Leuconostoc lactis EFEL005 (PMID 25935305, J Microbiol)
notes: The strain shows potential for probiotic applications and prebiotic effects in various media. Clinical trials are needed to confirm its efficacy.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed promise for a specific condition (IBD), but the findings were mixed across studies.

name: Leuconostoc mesenteroides
survivability_notes: Probiotic VITA-PB2 from fermented foods demonstrated effectiveness in reducing hangover symptoms and alcohol-induced oxidative stress.
key_rcts: Cao B et al. 2025 — Sustained reduction in blood ethanol concentrations (PMCID: PMC12299640, Nutrients)
notes: The probiotic strain Leuconostoc mesenteroides has shown promise in managing alcohol-induced oxidative stress and reducing hangover-related symptoms through a crossover study. Other studies suggest its potential to reduce infectious episodes in critically ill patients but with some methodological concerns.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed beneficial effects for hangover symptoms and alcohol metabolism, though no specific CFU dose or survival data is provided.

name: Levilactobacillus brevis
conditions: Symptomatology of Autism Spectrum Disorder and/or Attention Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder
survivability_notes: The probiotic strain is orally administered in a capsule form, and the effectiveness was observed based on blood estrogen levels. No specific notes about stability are provided.
key_rcts: Casertano M et al. 2024 — GABA-producing lactobacilli boost cognitive reactivity to negative mood without improving cognitive performance (PMID: 39163908, Brain Behav Immun)
Honda S et al. 2024 — Supplementation with a Probiotic Formula Having β-Glucuronidase Activity Modulates Serum Estrogen Levels in Healthy Peri- and Postmenopausal Women (PMID: 38742994, J Med Food)

Rojo-Marticella M et al. 2025 — Effect of Probiotics on the Symptomatology of Autism Spectrum Disorder and/or Attention Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder in Children and Adolescents: Pilot Study (PMID: 36198719, Res Child Adolesc Psychopathol)
notes: The abstracts provide evidence supporting the use of probiotics containing Levilactobacillus brevis in addressing estrogen levels, cognitive reactivity, and symptoms associated with autism spectrum disorder/attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder. However, all studies are observational or preliminary, lacking long-term intervention data.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two studies support the use of probiotics containing Levilactobacillus brevis for symptom modulation but one is a cross-over study which may have methodological limitations.

name: Ligilactobacillus salivarius
survivability_notes: The viability and stability of Ligilactobacillus salivarius were not specifically addressed in the provided abstracts.
key_rcts: JAMA Pediatr. 2022 Sep 1;176(9):860-866 — A multispecies probiotic reduced the risk of diarrhea (Preliminary, JAMA Pediatrics)
notes: The provided abstracts do not offer sufficient evidence to determine the viability, enteric coating requirements, effective CFU dosage, or specific clinical outcomes for Ligilactobacillus salivarius.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: There is a single RCT in probiotics for atopic dermatitis and another RCT in antibiotic-associated diarrhea that does not clearly support any effective dose or regimen.

name: Limosilactobacillus fermentum
survivability_notes: Longer-term studies including a higher number of infants are needed to determine whether the use of this probiotic during the early stages of life is an efficient way to reduce the incidence of GIs.
key_rcts: Pastor-Villaescusa et al. 2021 — Significant reduction in the incidence rate of GIs (PMCID: PMC8305821, Microorganisms)
notes: The meta-analysis found a significant reduction in the incidence of gastrointestinal infections with L. fermentum CECT5716, while another systematic review on atopic dermatitis found some efficacy with specific strains.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed significant benefit for reducing GI infections but findings were mixed across studies

name: Limosilactobacillus reuteri
survivability_notes: No information provided on stability or delivery methods.
key_rcts: Söderberg Veibäck G, et al. 2025 — adjunctive probiotic efficacy for inflammatory depression (PMID 40484149)
Gregori G, et al. 2024 — prevention of early postmenopausal bone loss in osteoporosis (N/A)
notes: Limited data on viabilities, efficacy dosage, or enteric coating is available from these abstracts.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: One RCT showed benefit for inflammatory depression and another for osteoporosis prevention but the findings were mixed across studies.

name: Pediococcus acidilactici
survivability_notes: No significant compositional changes were detected in fecal microbiota between probiotic and placebo.
key_rcts: Gutiérrez-Castrellón et al. 2021 — Probiotic improves symptomatic and viral clearance in Covid19 outpatients: a randomized, quadruple-blinded, placebo-controlled trial (PMID 35014600, Gut Microbes)
notes: One study showed potential efficacy for alleviating depressive symptoms and another studied its effects on Covid-19 outpatients, with no clear effect seen for upper respiratory infections.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two RCTs investigated the probiotic strain Pediococcus acidilactici in different health conditions but findings were mixed across studies.

name: Pediococcus pentosaceus
key_rcts: Moreno-Villares JM, et al. 2024 — Higher response rate and reduction in crying/fussing time (PMCID: PMC11527960, Eur J Pediatr)

Anvarifard P, et al. 2024 — Reductions in ventilator-associated pneumonia, nosocomial infections, ICU length of stay, hospital length of stay, ICU mortality, and hospital mortality (PMID: 38771159, Food Funct.)
notes: The strain has shown efficacy in reducing symptoms related to colic and pneumonia, but there is no data on its viability or stability. No specific CFU dose is mentioned for this strain.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT for infant colic showed benefit with Bifidobacterium longum KABP042 + Pediococcus pentosaceus KABP041 and a meta-analysis of probiotic use in critically ill patients (one RCT)

name: Propionibacterium freudenreichii subsp. shermanii
key_rcts: Konowałek Ł et al. 2025 — Reduced severity of core autism symptoms (41422017, Trials)
Tapiovaara L et al. 2016 — Safety analysis (26895235, Benef Microbes)
notes: The strain Propionibacterium freudenreichii has shown promise in reducing the severity of core autism symptoms through a multispecies probiotic, while no human clinical trials or meta-analyses are found on its efficacy against Salmonella infections.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed potential benefit for autism symptoms and one study investigated safety aspects of probiotic consumption.

name: Saccharomyces cerevisiae var. boulardii
survivability_notes: Mixed results for shelf stability.
key_rcts: Xie P et al. 2023 — Surface under the cumulative ranking (SUCRA) values for effectiveness in improving IBS Symptom Severity Scale, including Lactobacillus acidophilus DDS-1 as first ranked (PMCID: PMC10490209, Nutrients),
notes: Probiotic effectiveness was supported by various RCTs and meta-analyses, with key strains showing significant improvement in IBS symptom severity.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated strong: Multiple studies demonstrated efficacy of S. cerevisiae and Bacillus coagulans in treating IBS symptoms.

name: Kazachstania exigua
notes: The abstract discusses the role of Kazachstania exigua as a sourdough yeast, its metabolic properties, and potential functional benefits such as production of vitamins and inhibition of fungi. However, it does not provide information on specific conditions or doses for efficacy.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated unknown: No human clinical data found in these abstracts

name: Kazachstania turicensis
survivability_notes: No information available on the stability or delivery of Saccharomyces turicensis CAU Y1706.
key_rcts: Kim JH, Kim K, Kanjanasuntree R, Kim W. 2019. Kazachstania turicensis CAU Y1706 ameliorates atopic dermatitis by regulation of the gut-skin axis (J Dairy Sci). PMID: 30738679; Journal: J Dairy Sci
notes: This study provides evidence that K. turicensis CAU Y1706 may be effective against atopic dermatitis when administered orally.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefit for AD, but findings were mixed across studies as there are multiple strains and conditions studied in the literature

name: Kazachstania unispora
survivability_notes: Probiotic yeasts confirmed viable in final drinks and showed no influence on glycaemic response.
key_rcts: Wang et al. 2025 — KU2 ameliorates patulin-induced intestinal injury (138129), J Hazard Mater
notes: Studies focus on K. unispora strains and their ability to improve gut microbiota homeostasis and reduce gastrointestinal disorders.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed beneficial effects for intestinal injury, but other studies only support probiotic potential without specific doses or clinical outcomes.

name: Enterococcus faecalis
survivability_notes: Probiotics in enteric-coated formulations were administered via oral route.
key_rcts: Marashi A et al. 2024 — gut microbiome alterations associated with gastric cancer (PMID 39170038)
Lu C et al. 2025 — efficacy of probiotic regimens for allergic rhinitis (PMID 39837158)
notes: The evidence landscape shows both promising results from clinical trials and preliminary data on other health conditions. The effectiveness of Enterococcus faecalis is mainly discussed in relation to gastrointestinal symptoms and cancer treatment.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed benefit for gastric cancer and another meta-analysis supported the use of probiotics in allergic rhinitis, but findings were mixed across studies.

name: Streptococcus salivarius
survivability_notes: The probiotic strain Streptococcus salivarius K12 is provided as lozenges and demonstrated to be effective in reducing the incidence, onset, and duration of oral mucositis associated with radiotherapy for malignant head and neck tumors. It also showed promise in preventing or treating sore throat.
key_rcts: Peng X et al. 2024 — Significant reduction in severe OM (PMID 38215354, JCO)
Wilcox CR et al. 2019 — Prevention of streptococcal pharyngitis and protection against chronic adenoiditis exacerbation (PMID 30616011, CMI)
notes: Studies on Streptococcus salivarius M18 for antigingivitis and antiplaque effects are limited, as only one study is available which found a significant decrease in gingival inflammation.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: two randomized controlled trials demonstrated the efficacy of SsK12 in reducing oral mucositis and preventing streptococcal pharyngitis.

name: Streptococcus thermophilus
key_rcts: Am J Obstet Gynecol MFM. 2023 Nov;5(11):101148 — Effects of maternal probiotic supplementation on breast milk microbiome and infant gut microbiome and health: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (PMID 37660760, Am J Obstet Gynecol MFM)
notes: Two abstracts describe the effectiveness of multi-strain probiotics containing Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, and Streptococcus thermophilus for treating IBS-D. No specific dose or viability information is provided.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: one RCT showed significant improvement in IBS symptoms but findings were mixed across studies

name: Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. lactis
survivability_notes: The probiotic mixture was administered in tablet form and the delivery method is not specified.
key_rcts: Gómez-Pérez AM et al. 2024 — Fecal microbiota transplantation for T2D (PMCID: PMC11510444, Nutrients)
Ma G et al. 2024 — Probiotic administration during pregnancy and gut microbiota diversity (PMCID: PMC11237737, Microbiol Spectra)
notes: The probiotic strain Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. lactis is used in different contexts (fecal microbiota transplantation for diabetes and probiotic administration during pregnancy).
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated preliminary: only one abstract provides information about a probiotic strain effect in humans. The other studies do not provide clear information on the effectiveness or dosing.

name: Lactococcus lactis
survivability_notes: Data is limited to in vitro and animal studies, no mention of enteric coating.
key_rcts: Abdallah Reda A. et al. 2025 — Probiotic therapies for T2DM (PMID 40646644)
notes: Limited human clinical data is available. The effects on menopausal symptoms are supported by a pilot study but no specific strain was tested.
evidence_tier_rationale: Rated moderate: only one RCT evaluated the effectiveness of probiotics in patients with type 2 diabetes, and mixed findings were observed across studies.
